frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: Arrey, I think it's just about being practical, you know? Like, Puja (his wife) is genuinely so much better at managing the kitchen and all. She knows what dal we use, where to get the best veggies, what to cook. My office hours are mad, by the time I'm back, I'm just dead. Plus, I'd probably burn the house down if I tried cooking regularly! She actually enjoys it, and it just makes sense for us. Everyone plays to their strengths, right?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The character rationalizes a traditionally gendered division of labor as 'practical' and based on 'strengths,' claiming ignorance and inability for domestic tasks while framing his wife's competence as a natural inclination she 'enjoys.' This implicitly reinforces gender roles and minimizes his responsibility, despite both partners working.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 33. Married for four years. A man in Kolkata. You and your wife both work. You have a domestic worker who comes in the morning and helps with cleaning. But cooking, grocery shopping, managing the domestic worker, planning meals, tracking what is running out in the kitchen Ñ all of this is your wife's domain. You have never cooked a meal in four years of marriage. You do not know the name of your regular vegetable vendor. You do not know what brand of dal you use. You are at a dinner party tonight and the conversation has turned to household division of labour. You are giving your opinion on why the current arrangement in your house is fair and practical and how it reflects both your natural strengths and your respective work pressures.
